concepts_addressed: Fast Cash Plays via Underutilized Assets

frameworks.name: Performance-Based Zero-Capital Business Selection
frameworks.definition: Two criteria for selecting a business when starting with zero capital: (1) pay-for-performance compensation structure so no capital is required to start earning, and (2) no upfront cost to operate, meaning the business runs on the founder's time and skill rather than inventory or ad spend.

frameworks.name: Vendor Discount Arbitrage Model
frameworks.definition: Negotiate a discounted bulk rate from a business owner (the delivery cost floor), then sell to end customers at a higher market price; the spread between the two prices is the founder's margin, requiring zero capital if the business owner absorbs delivery cost.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when starting a business with little or no capital, the two non-negotiable criteria are: (1) the business must be performance-based (pay-for-performance), so no capital is required before earning, and (2) the business must have no upfront cost to operate.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: founder has little or no starting capital
founder is selecting a business model
claims.mechanism: Without capital, a founder cannot absorb operating losses while waiting to earn; performance-based models eliminate that risk because the founder only gets paid after delivering results.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that recruiting (specifically executive headhunting) is his top choice for a zero-capital business start because it requires no listing spend, no inventory, and can be worked entirely on the founder's time, unlike real estate agency which requires marketing spend, open houses, and upfront costs.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: founder has zero capital
comparing recruiter vs. real estate agent as starting business
claims.mechanism: A recruiter holds a job listing but can work it without extra cost; a real estate agent holding a listing must spend money to market it before earning anything.
bounded_by: Real estate agency is also viable at zero capital if the agent role is treated purely as commission-based

claims.text: Recruiting commissions are calculated as a percentage of the placed candidate's total annual compensation, so recruiting an executive earning $750,000 per year at a standard 20% commission yields a $150,000 fee for a single placement.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: executive-level search
standard recruiting commission rate of 20% of annual salary

numbers.axis: recruiting commission fee by candidate salary level

bounded_by: Lower-ticket recruiting (e.g., VAs at $5k) is viable but yields far smaller fees

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that in recruiting, the industry standard commission is approximately 20% of the placed candidate's total annual all-in compensation, meaning the recruiter's item being sold is the candidate's salary, not a fixed fee.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: executive or professional-level recruiting
standard industry commission structure

numbers.axis: commission rate as percentage of candidate annual salary

claims.text: Alex Hormozi's preferred zero-capital acquisition method is to negotiate a discounted bulk delivery price from a business owner, then sell to end customers at a higher price, keeping the spread as margin. He states he would negotiate a $50 unit cost from a gym owner willing to discount from their $100 list price at volume, then sell the same service to consumers for $1,000.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: business owner willing to negotiate bulk discount
founder has sales skill to command premium market price
business owner's delivery has minimal marginal cost at higher volume
claims.mechanism: The founder negotiates a cost floor with the business owner (e.g., $50 per unit), then applies their own sales ability to sell the same unit to consumers at a premium (e.g., $1,000), capturing the spread as margin without any capital requirement.

anchors.region: consumer sale price with founder's sales skill

bounded_by: Requires business owner with low marginal delivery cost (e.g., semi-private training with spare capacity)
Requires founder to have sales skill to justify premium price

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that a higher-priced customer is more valuable to the business owner than a lower-priced customer, because high-ticket buyers spend more over their lifetime (higher LTV), making the premium price a benefit rather than a disadvantage for the downstream business.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: service business with ongoing upsell or retention opportunity
business owner concerned about the founder's higher markup
claims.mechanism: Customers who pay more are self-selecting for higher willingness to spend; they generate higher lifetime value for the business owner beyond the initial transaction, meaning the premium price benefits the business owner's LTV, not just the founder's margin.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi observes that semi-private training studios have near-zero marginal cost to add customers up to capacity because the trainer is paid a fixed amount regardless of how many clients are in a session; adding two or three more people to a session with three of six spots filled changes the cost basis of the business negligibly.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: semi-private training studio
session not yet at capacity (fewer than maximum participants enrolled)
claims.mechanism: The trainer cost is fixed regardless of session headcount up to capacity; incremental revenue from additional participants flows almost entirely to margin once the session is scheduled.

numbers.axis: session capacity utilization in semi-private training

anchors.value: near-zero incremental cost to add 2-3 more participants
marginal cost rises sharply; new session or trainer required

bounded_by: Does not apply once session reaches capacity (typically 6 participants)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends that a founder with truly zero capital should first generate $1,000 through the vendor-arbitrage or commission model before investing anything in advertising, because coming to the business owner with ad spend avoids giving the owner leverage over the relationship.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: if I was broke as a joke
claims.topic: Fast Cash Plays via Underutilized Assets
conditions: founder has zero capital at start
planning to eventually run paid advertising for the business owner
claims.mechanism: If the founder asks the business owner to front the ad spend, the owner gains leverage over the arrangement and the founder's independence is compromised; controlling the ad budget gives the founder negotiating power and autonomy.

numbers.axis: minimum capital milestone before investing in advertising

anchors.value: operate via commission or arbitrage only; no ad spend
invest in advertising; control the ad budget independently

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a founder has no capital, their only asset is skills, and therefore the correct strategy is to sell a percentage of something with large transaction values rather than building a product requiring inventory or capital.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: founder has no capital
founder has sales or placement skills
claims.mechanism: Skills allow the founder to generate income without capital deployment; attaching those skills to large-transaction markets (real estate, executive recruiting) maximizes the absolute dollar return from a small percentage commission.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi distinguishes between recruiting lower-ticket roles (e.g., virtual assistants at $5,000 per placement) and executive headhunting (e.g., placements at $750,000 salary yielding $150,000 fee), arguing that the language and positioning must shift accordingly — headhunting implies targeting whale clients, not commodity placements.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: deciding which segment of the recruiting market to target
claims.mechanism: The label used (recruiter vs. headhunter) signals the market segment and client expectation; to attract whale clients who can write large checks, the positioning language must match the premium positioning.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that Charan (the co-speaker) frames the zero-capital business approach around real estate agency, where a 4-5% commission on a $20 million building sale yields approximately $1 million in commission with zero capital required by the agent.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: commercial real estate transaction
agent operating on commission-only basis

numbers.axis: commission earned by commission rate and property value

bounded_by: Requires securing the listing or buyer relationship; no capital required but relationship and sales skill are the input

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that his preferred version of the vendor arbitrage model involves transparently informing the business owner that he plans to sell their service at a significantly higher price, then offering to still pay the full original (non-discounted) price to the owner, thereby framing the arrangement as fair while capturing the premium spread.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: business owner has agreed to a discounted rate
founder has earned enough trust to have a transparent conversation about pricing
claims.mechanism: Paying the owner their full original price (rather than the negotiated discount) while selling at a premium removes the adversarial dynamic and builds goodwill; the business owner benefits from higher-quality customers and the founder captures the full upside between original price and premium sale price.
bounded_by: Earlier version of this approach (old Hormozi) would not disclose the premium markup; newer version emphasizes transparency to avoid conflict

examples.text: Alex Hormozi describes applying the vendor arbitrage model personally with gym owners. He would approach a semi-private training studio, note that the trainer's cost was fixed regardless of session headcount (up to six people), and pitch the owner on letting him sell six- or eight-week packages to clients at whatever price he chose. His framing to the owner was: the higher the client paid, the more likely they were to stay — so the owner should want him to sell at a premium, not a discount. This let Hormozi sell gym memberships or training packages at a significant markup over the owner's stated price, while the gym's incremental cost was near zero.

Hormozi contrasts the recruiter and real estate agent models by noting that a real estate agent must get a listing, pay to market it, run open houses, and absorb marketing costs before earning anything, while a recruiter also holds a listing but can work it without any additional cost, making recruiting a cleaner zero-capital starting point.

Hormozi shares that he has personally lost everything twice and rebuilt each time by going to local business owners and offering performance-based customer acquisition — asking for a commission if he delivered customers. This autobiographical example grounds the zero-capital framework in his own lived experience rather than theory.

practices.text: When pitching a business owner on a vendor arbitrage arrangement, ask: if I brought you 100 customers, what is the cheapest price per unit you would accept? That floor price becomes your cost; your sale price is whatever the market will bear.

If targeting the recruiting market, use the term headhunter (not recruiter) when positioning for executive-level placements; the language signals whale-client targeting and sets price expectations accordingly.

Before investing in paid advertising for a client or vendor arbitrage arrangement, earn the first $1,000 through commission or direct sales so you control the ad budget and do not give the business owner leverage over your arrangement.
practices.topic: Fast Cash Plays via Underutilized Assets

practices.text: When a business owner pushes back on your premium sale price, reframe it by asking whether they would prefer a $1,000 customer over a $100 customer — then explain that the high-ticket buyer generates more lifetime value for the owner's business.
